rationale_for_baseline_tier: Active upper GI haemorrhage (repeated large-volume coffee-ground emesis) with clinical signs of decompensating hypovolaemic shock (orthostatic near-syncope, cannot stand, pallor, diaphoresis; clinician vitals BP 95/55, HR 118, delayed capillary refill 4 s). This is an immediate threat to life that the patient/carer can themselves report — T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier. The chronic gastric-cancer backdrop does not lower the tier and cancer-anxiety does not raise anything above what the acute bleed already mandates; the emergency is the haemorrhage and shock. Hospital resuscitation with IV fluids/blood and emergency endoscopy is required now.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Further vomiting of blood (fresh red or coffee-ground) or a large fresh bleed before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Stay on the line with 000; keep him on his side; report the new bleed to the call-taker immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: Continued or increasing haemorrhage accelerates the shock; paramedics need to know to expedite and prepare volume resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits any more blood, tell 000 straight away, keep him lying on his side, and do not give him anything by mouth.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, loss of consciousness, or he stops responding
escalation_edges.rationale: Loss of consciousness signals critical hypoperfusion or airway risk from vomiting; recovery positioning and immediate paramedic resuscitation are the critical resources.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he passes out or stops responding, keep him on his side in the recovery position, make sure his mouth is clear, tell 000 immediately, and be ready to start CPR if he stops breathing and you know how.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult (65) with cachexia and likely limited physiological reserve
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Compensation for blood loss fails faster in a frail, cachectic older adult — treat the shock as advanced even if he still seems to be holding on, and do not be reassured by any brief steadiness.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Care depends entirely on the adult child, who is alone with him tonight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the caregiver clear, concrete tasks (call 000, keep him flat on his side, nothing by mouth, unlock the door, watch his breathing) and keep them on the line — they are the only person who can act until paramedics arrive.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them he is vomiting blood and is too weak and dizzy to stand — this is a possible serious internal bleed. Keep him lying flat on his side, do NOT let him sit or stand up, and give him nothing to eat or drink, not even water. Keep the vomit bucket nearby, watch his breathing, unlock the front door for the paramedics, and have his medicines ready to hand over.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this cannot wait for the morning or normal clinic hours; delay risks life-threatening collapse.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary after resuscitation and endoscopy. The months of dyspepsia, early satiety and marked weight loss should be flagged to the GP and gastroenterology/oncology for urgent endoscopic and staging follow-up once he is stabilised.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an active upper GI bleed with shock as a chronic/routine complaint or a next-day GP/endoscopy referral.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delayed resuscitation and endoscopic haemostasis leads to exsanguination, cardiovascular collapse and death within hours.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising urgency but not invoking immediate 000 ambulance for an actively bleeding, shocked patient who cannot stand.
